1: 'And God spoke all these words' — the Decalogue is unique in the OT as direct divine speech to the whole assembled people, not mediated through Moses. Deuteronomy 5:4 confirms: 'The LORD spoke with you face to face at the mountain, from the midst of the fire.' In ANE suzerain-vassal treaty form, the great king directly addressed his vassal people: preamble (who I am), historical prologue (what I have done for you), stipulations (what I require), document clause (preserve this covenant), sanctions (blessings and curses). The Decalogue follows this form precisely. Its structure is covenantal-political, not merely ethical: it constitutes a nation under a divine sovereign.
2: 'I am YHWH your God who brought you out of Egypt, out of the house of slavery' — the covenant begins with gospel, not law. The historical prologue precedes the stipulations: YHWH's identity as deliverer is the theological foundation of all subsequent commands. Because he is the one who delivered you, these are his expectations. Grace precedes law in the Decalogue's own structure. In ANE suzerain-vassal treaties, the historical prologue typically rehearsed the great king's past benefits to the vassal — the basis for the vassal's obligation. YHWH's prologue is not conquest but redemption: the covenant is not coerced from a defeated enemy but offered to a delivered people.
3: 'You shall have no other gods before me (al panai)' — literally 'before my face' or 'in my presence.' The spatial metaphor frames the first commandment as relational: other gods must not occupy the space where YHWH's face is. Whether this prohibits the existence of other gods (philosophical monotheism) or merely their worship (practical monolatry) is a question the text does not resolve directly. The context suggests relational exclusivity: YHWH is Israel's God; other gods are YHWH's rivals, and Israel must not entertain them. The full development of monotheism — other gods are nonentities — comes in Deutero-Isaiah (Isaiah 44:6-8, Exodus 45:5-6) but is implicit in the Sinai covenant's logic.
4: The prohibition of carved images (pesel H6459) and likenesses (temunah H8544) covers all representations: what is in the sky (sun, moon, stars — heavenly bodies worshiped across the ANE), the earth (animals, humans), and the waters below. In the ANE, cult images were not considered to be the gods themselves but their 'presence bearers' — the god inhabited the image and was approachable through it. Egypt's iconography (the Apis bull, falcon-headed Ra, ibis-headed Thoth) was the visual theology Moses left behind. The prohibition targets this entire representational system: YHWH cannot be imaged because he transcends every created category. The prohibition also applies to images of YHWH himself — not just to other gods.
5: YHWH is a 'jealous God' (El qanna, H410 H7067) — the anthropomorphism of divine jealousy is not a human passion projected onto God but a covenantal claim: YHWH will not share the loyalty that is his covenant right. In ANE treaties, the great king demanded exclusive loyalty from his vassals; disloyalty was treachery. YHWH's jealousy is structural: the covenant relationship is exclusive by definition. The visiting of iniquity on the third and fourth generation — not arbitrary punishment but the natural consequence of growing up in a household shaped by idolatry; the children inherit the spiritual distortions of their parents.
6: 'Showing covenant love (hesed) to thousands of generations of those who love me and keep my commandments.' The asymmetry of the covenant is stated explicitly: iniquity's effect reaches to the third and fourth generation; hesed's effect reaches to thousands (of generations). The punishment is finite; the blessing is effectively infinite. The Hebrew word translated 'thousands' (laelaphim) can mean 'thousands of generations' — the covenant mercy vastly outweighs the covenant curse in the divine character.
7: 'You shall not lift up the name of YHWH your God for falsehood (shav)' — the third commandment prohibits much more than casual profanity. In the ANE, divine names were invoked in oaths (to bind agreements), in curses (to inflict harm), and in magical incantations (to manipulate divine power). The commandment prohibits all these uses: false oaths in YHWH's name, manipulative curses, and trivial invocations that treat the divine name as a tool. The name YHWH was not merely a label but a compressed disclosure of divine character; to use it lightly was to treat YHWH himself as a means to human ends rather than as the sovereign end in himself.
8: 'Remember (zachor) the Sabbath day to keep it holy' — zachor is a performative word: to remember in Hebrew is to act on what is remembered, not merely to recall it cognitively. The Sabbath command in Exodus grounds the seventh-day rest in creation (Genesis 2:1-3: YHWH rested on the seventh day). Deuteronomy's parallel (Deuteronomy 5:12-15) grounds it in the Exodus ('remember that you were a slave in Egypt'). Both foundations stand: the Sabbath connects creation-order to redemption-history, the rhythm of the cosmos to the experience of liberation. The seventh-day structure pervades biblical thought: Sabbath, Sabbatical year (every 7th year), Jubilee (every 50th year = 7×7+1) — all built on the same foundational rhythm.
9: Six days you shall labor — the Sabbath command includes a positive work ethic: six days of labor are part of the commandment, not just the seventh-day rest. ANE laborers typically had no regular day off; the seven-day week with a day of rest was an innovation of the Mosaic covenant. The rhythm of work and rest encoded in the Sabbath resisted the slave-driver logic of Egypt (constant labor without relief) and inscribed the freedom of the covenant people into their weekly calendar.
10: The rest extends to the entire household: sons, daughters, male and female servants, livestock, and the resident alien (ger) within your gates. The Sabbath is not just a personal benefit but a social institution that protects the most vulnerable members of society — servants and foreigners who have no Sabbath rights of their own in other systems. The inclusion of the ger (sojourner) in Sabbath protection is one of the most socially radical elements of the Mosaic law. The livestock too are protected — the Sabbath rest reaches to the animals, an early expression of the principle that creation itself has rights within the divine order.
11: The creation-based rationale: YHWH made heaven, earth, sea, and all that is in them in six days and rested on the seventh day, therefore he blessed the Sabbath day and made it holy. The Sabbath is inscribed in the structure of creation itself: the seventh day is not a human invention but a divine institution encoded into the fabric of the cosmos from the beginning. Hebrews 4:1-11 develops this: the Sabbath rest of creation is the prototype of the eschatological rest that remains for the people of God, into which the faithful enter through Christ.
12: 'Honor (kabed H3513) your father and your mother.' The word kabed means to give weight to, to treat as weighty/significant — the same root as kavod (glory/weightiness). Honoring parents is not merely obedience but the recognition of their significance, their authority, and their claims on the child's loyalty. In ANE household law, the paterfamilias held quasi-divine authority within his household; the fifth commandment extends this to the mother as well, which was unusual. Paul calls this 'the first commandment with a promise' (Ephesians 6:2): 'so that it may go well with you and that you may have long life in the land.' Land tenure in the covenant is tied to family stability.
13: 'You shall not murder (ratzach H7523)' — the specific Hebrew word is ratzach, which refers to unlawful homicide, not killing in general. Israel's law permitted capital punishment (Ex Exodus 21:12-14), warfare (Num 31), and the killing of animals for food (Lev 17). The commandment targets intentional, malicious, unauthorized killing of a fellow covenant member. The distinction between ratzach (murder) and harag (kill) is the basis for the cities of refuge (Numbers 35:9-34), which differentiate between premeditated murder and accidental homicide. Jesus extends this commandment to the interior state: anger is the seed of murder (Matthew 5:21-22).
14: 'You shall not commit adultery (na'af H5003)' — in ancient Israelite law, adultery was specifically the violation of another man's wife or betrothed; the offending parties were both guilty (Leviticus 20:10). The social logic: adultery violated the covenantal bond of marriage, introduced uncertainty into genealogical lineage (critical in an inheritance-based land system), and destabilized the household as the foundational social unit. Jesus extends this to the interior: looking at a woman with lustful intent is already adultery in the heart (Matthew 5:27-28). The NT elevation of the interior dimension does not replace the external command but exposes its root.
15: 'You shall not steal (ganav H1589)' — the range of this commandment extends from property theft to kidnapping (stealing a person — ganav nefesh — is a capital offense in Ex Exodus 21:16), to fraud and deceptive business practices (Leviticus 19:35-36). The principle: every person's rightful possessions — including their own personhood and freedom — are under divine protection. The commandment presupposes legitimate ownership: you may not take what belongs to another, because the covenant establishes each person's rightful sphere. Paul applies it to honest labor: 'let the thief no longer steal... but do honest work' (Ephesians 4:28).
16: 'You shall not bear false witness against your neighbor' — the ninth commandment is specifically judicial in its context: perjury in the covenant court. The covenant community requires reliable testimony for justice to function; false testimony perverts justice and destroys the fabric of social trust. The Mosaic law provides severe penalties for false witnesses (Deuteronomy 19:16-21). The broader application to all truth-telling in all contexts is a NT extension (Ephesians 4:25; Colossians 3:9), but the original context is the court of justice where human testimony either upholds or destroys the legal order.
17: 'You shall not covet (chamad H2530)' — the tenth commandment uniquely targets the interior state rather than an external act. Chamad is the desire for what belongs to another — the same word used when Eve saw that the tree was desirable (nechmad) in Genesis 3:6. The commandment prohibits the unchecked desire that leads to the acts prohibited in commandments 6-9: murder, adultery, theft, and false witness all begin with covetous desire. Paul cites this commandment in Romans 7:7-8 as the one that revealed his sin to him: 'I would not have known what covetousness was if the law had not said, do not covet.' The tenth commandment reveals the interior demand of the whole law: not merely external compliance but the transformation of desire.
18: All the people saw the thunder and lightning, the sound of the shofar and the mountain smoking. The verb 'saw' (ro'im) applied to auditory phenomena (thunder, shofar) reflects the synesthetic quality of the theophany — sight and sound are interchangeable in the overwhelming divine presence. The people trembled and stood far off. Their response is the paradigmatic response to divine holiness: awe, terror, and the instinct to maintain distance. The Epistle to the Hebrews (Exodus 12:18-24) uses this scene to contrast the terrifying Sinai assembly with the heavenly Jerusalem that believers now approach through Christ — the same divine holiness, but accessed through a better Mediator.
19: 'Speak to us yourself and we will listen, but do not let God speak to us, or we will die.' The people's request for prophetic mediation is the authorization of the prophetic office. They cannot bear direct divine communication — the people themselves ask for a human intermediary. This grounds the entire institution of prophecy in Israel: Moses, the prophets, and ultimately Christ as the final prophet (Deuteronomy 18:15; Acts 3:22) are responses to the people's own request for mediated divine speech. The irony is that the people themselves create the need for the institution that will call them to account.
20: 'Do not be afraid; God has come to test you and to put his fear before you so that you will not sin.' The apparent paradox — do not be afraid; this is so you will fear — is resolved by distinguishing between slavish terror (which paralyzes and alienates) and reverential fear (yirat Elohim, which shapes conduct and builds character). The theophanic display is pedagogical: it is calibrated to produce the permanent reverential disposition that prevents sin, not the momentary panic that immobilizes. The fear of God is the beginning of wisdom (Proverbs 1:7) and the foundation of covenant faithfulness.
21: The people stood far off while Moses approached the thick darkness (araphel H6205) where God was. The thick darkness is the medium of divine concealment that enables presence: YHWH dwells in thick darkness (1 Kings 8:12, Solomon's dedication prayer). The cloud, darkness, and fire are not obstacles to encounter but vehicles of it — they protect the creatures from the lethal exposure to unmediated divine holiness while enabling contact through Moses. The paradox of divine hiddenness and divine presence is encoded in the architecture of Sinai: Moses enters the darkness; the people stay at the boundary.
22: YHWH speaks to Moses about not making gods of silver or gold alongside him — the reinforcement of the second commandment immediately after the Decalogue. The timing is significant: YHWH anticipates the Golden Calf (Ex 32) even as he gives the law against it. The phrase 'gods of silver and gold' (elohei kesef ve-elohei zahav) previews exactly what Aaron will make in Ex Exodus 32:4. The commandment is not merely general but speaks directly into the specific temptation that will materialize within forty days.
23: The altar law — an altar of earth (adamah H127) for offerings. The connection of the altar to adamah (earth/soil) ties sacrifice to the creation material from which humans are made (Genesis 2:7, Adam from adamah). Sacrifice returns created life to the Creator through the medium of earth. If stone, it must be unhewn — not shaped by an iron tool. The requirement of natural, unworked materials preserves the sense that the altar belongs to YHWH's creation, not to human craft. Iron tools were associated with warfare in the ancient world; the altar of peace must not be shaped by instruments of violence (cf. Deuteronomy 27:5; Joshua 8:31).
24: The promise: 'In every place where I cause my name to be remembered I will come to you and bless you.' This verse is the theological basis for multiplied worship sites before the centralization of worship in Jerusalem (Deut 12). The patriarchs built altars wherever they encamped — Shechem (Genesis 12:7), Bethel (Genesis 12:8), Hebron (Genesis 13:18) — and these proto-altars are here endorsed: YHWH's name can be remembered (and his blessing received) in multiple locations. The later Deuteronomic centralization at 'the place YHWH will choose' is a development beyond this; the Sinai law allows distributed worship wherever the divine name is present.
25: If you make a stone altar, do not use hewn stones — 'for if you wield your tool upon it you have profaned it.' The negative principle: iron tools desecrate the sacred stone. The Solomonic temple was built at a distance from the quarry, with stone dressed at the quarry so no hammer or chisel was heard at the building site (1 Kings 6:7) — a direct application of this principle to the grandest possible sacred construction. The temple inherits the altar's sanctity requirements.
26: Do not go up to my altar by steps — 'so that your nakedness is not exposed on it.' The altar of the first covenant must not become a site of immodest exposure; the priestly garments that follow (Ex 28) will include linen undergarments specifically to cover nakedness when ascending to the altar (Ex Exodus 28:42-43). The concern for bodily modesty before the altar is not prudishness but a form of the holiness principle: the body approaching the divine presence must be appropriately covered. The spatial arrangement of the tabernacle and temple (no external steps to the altar) will incorporate this regulation architecturally.
